question: Explain why benzene undergoes substitution rather than addition.
prompt: Benzene contains a stable delocalised pi system. Addition would destroy this _____, so substitution is favoured because the aromatic system is retained.
full_answer: Benzene contains a stable delocalised pi system. Addition would destroy this delocalisation, so substitution is favoured because the aromatic system is retained.

question: Explain why benzene undergoes substitution rather than addition.
prompt: Benzene has a delocalised pi electron system which is very _____. Addition would break the delocalisation and reduce the stability. Therefore benzene tends to undergo substitution so that the delocalised system is retained.
full_answer: Benzene has a delocalised pi electron system which is very stable. Addition would break the delocalisation and reduce the stability. Therefore benzene tends to undergo substitution so that the delocalised system is retained.

question: Explain why benzene is less likely than an alkene to undergo addition reactions.
prompt: Benzene has a _____ pi system and is stabilised by aromaticity. An addition reaction would break this delocalisation, so addition is less favourable than substitution.
full_answer: Benzene has a delocalised pi system and is stabilised by aromaticity. An addition reaction would break this delocalisation, so addition is less favourable than substitution.
